title: Dog Stiff Gait After Heavy Exercise: Causes and Home Care
meta_desc: Is your dog stiff after heavy exercise? Learn about muscle soreness, urgency levels, and why a video helps your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
What is Post-Exercise Stiffness?

Just like humans, dogs can experience muscle fatigue or delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) after a period of intense activity. This stiff gait is often caused by micro-tears in the muscle fibers or minor inflammation in the joints. It is especially common in dogs that are older, overweight, or those who have sudden bursts of high energy after a sedentary week.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a general stiff gait after heavy exercise is Low. If your dog is still eating, drinking, and able to move on all four legs, you can typically monitor them at home with rest. However, if the stiffness does not improve within 24 hours, or if your dog is non-weight-bearing on a limb, you should schedule a routine veterinary appointment.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a video of your dog’s gait is one of the most helpful tools for a veterinary professional. A video allows us to see exactly how your dog is placing their paws and which joints seem restricted. Because dogs often hide their pain or move differently due to adrenaline at the clinic, a video of the behavior happening at home helps us pinpoint if the issue is muscular, joint-related, or neurological.

faqs.question: How long should my dog rest after showing stiffness?
faqs.answer: It is best to enforce 24 to 48 hours of restricted activity, meaning short leashed bathroom breaks only and no running or jumping.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like Ibuprofen or Tylenol, as these can be highly toxic to dogs. Always consult your vet for pet-safe anti-inflammatories.

faqs.question: When does stiffness become a medical priority?
faqs.answer: If your dog is vocalizing in pain, unable to stand, or if you notice dragging of the paws (knuckling), you should seek veterinary care immediately.
